text:
SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND WHOLE-BODY TECHNETIUM-99m BONE SCINTIGRAPHY

CLINICAL HISTORY: 69-year-old male with high-risk locally advanced prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M0 at diagnosis, PSA 87.4 ng/mL at diagnosis). Treated with androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide acetate) plus abiraterone/prednisone initiated approximately 24 months ago, with definitive pelvic IMRT (78 Gy to prostate/seminal vesicles, 54 Gy to pelvic nodes) completed approximately 21 months ago. Presenting for routine surveillance. Current PSA: 0.3 ng/mL. Testosterone: castrate.

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Multidetector CT performed with intravenous contrast (100 mL iohexol, portal venous phase). Scout images obtained. Axial images acquired through the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with coronal and sagittal reconstructions. Slice thickness: 3 mm. Oral contrast administered.

Bone scintigraphy: Intravenous administration of approximately 25 mCi Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP). Whole-body planar images obtained at 3 hours post-injection in anterior and posterior projections. SPECT/CT of the pelvis and thoracolumbar spine obtained as a supplement to planar imaging given prior nodal disease at diagnosis.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan obtained approximately 24 months prior at time of initial staging.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:
Lungs: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules identified. No parenchymal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally.
Mediastinum: The mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size without pericardial effusion.
Chest wall / osseous thorax: Visualized osseous structures of the thorax are unremarkable. No lytic or sclerotic lesions identified. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, age appropriate.

CT ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.
Gallbladder: Unremarkable, without stones or wall thickening.
Pancreas: Normal pancreatic morphology and attenuation. No ductal dilatation.
Spleen: Normal in size and homogeneous. No focal lesions.
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses. (Note: patient on abiraterone; adrenal suppression expected.)
Kidneys: Both kidneys are of normal size. A 4 mm simple-appearing cortical cyst in the right kidney, unchanged from prior. No hydronephrosis bilaterally. Normal cortical enhancement. No perirenal fat stranding.
Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No para-aortic or peri-caval lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement (all nodes < 8 mm in short axis above the aortic bifurcation).
Bowel: Nondistended loops of small and large bowel without wall thickening, obstruction, or free air. No mesenteric masses.
Vascular: Aorta is normal in caliber throughout without aneurysmal dilatation. Mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the infrarenal aorta and iliac vessels, consistent with age and known cardiovascular risk history.

CT PELVIS:
Prostate bed: The prostate is markedly diminished in size consistent with expected radiation-induced atrophy and ongoing androgen suppression. Post-radiation fibrotic changes are seen in the prostate bed with some heterogeneous signal and ill-defined margins, representing expected post-treatment sequelae. No discrete nodular soft tissue mass is identified. The seminal vesicle regions demonstrate post-treatment fibrotic change bilaterally, more pronounced on the right consistent with prior seminal vesicle involvement and directed radiation.
Bladder: The bladder is partially filled and demonstrates mild irregular wall thickening posteriorly, likely representing post-radiation cystitis change, similar in appearance to baseline. No discrete intraluminal mass.
Rectum: Mild rectal wall thickening is noted anteriorly, consistent with radiation proctitis changes. Similar to prior examination.
Pelvic lymph nodes: The right obturator lymph node previously measuring 1.8 cm has significantly decreased in size, now measuring approximately 0.6 cm in short axis and demonstrating preserved fat hilum morphology. The previously noted right iliac chain lymph nodes (0.9 cm and 0.7 cm at diagnosis) are no longer clearly identifiable as discrete enlarged nodes; residual small lymph nodes up to 0.5 cm in the right external iliac region, which may represent treated/regressing nodes or normal-caliber nodes. No new pelvic lymphadenopathy. No new inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Osseous pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic lesions identified. Bilateral hip joints are maintained. No suspicious cortical or marrow changes.

WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN:
There is normal physiologic tracer distribution throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton. Symmetric renal excretion. No focal areas of abnormal increased or decreased radiotracer uptake to suggest active osseous metastatic disease. There is mildly increased uptake at the bilateral sacroiliac joints and lumbar facet joints, consistent with degenerative changes. The previously intact bone scan from the initial staging study remains the comparative basis; no interval development of new osseous lesions is identified on either planar or SPECT/CT images.

SPECT/CT pelvis: Mild heterogeneous uptake in the prostate bed region is noted, which is nonspecific and most consistent with post-treatment changes and radiation fibrosis. No focal discrete lesion with the pattern of osseous metastatic disease.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on this surveillance examination. No pulmonary, hepatic, adrenal, or other visceral metastases identified.

2. Bone scan negative for osseous metastatic disease, with no new lesions and no suspicious foci on supplemental SPECT/CT. Degenerative changes only.

3. Marked interval decrease in size of the previously enlarged right obturator lymph node (1.8 cm at diagnosis, now 0.6 cm), consistent with favorable treatment response to ADT, abiraterone, and pelvic radiation. Previously identified right iliac chain nodes no longer clearly enlarged.

4. Expected post-radiation changes in the prostate bed and adjacent structures, including radiation-related changes of the posterior bladder wall and anterior rectal wall.

5. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

6. Overall assessment: findings are consistent with ongoing favorable systemic and locoregional response to current treatment regimen. No imaging evidence of disease recurrence or new metastatic sites at this time.

Reporting radiologist: [Attending Radiologist]
Facility: [Institution]
Examination date: [Date added programmatically]
